business_goals: Control movement, strategy, and operations of troops in real-time
Support a Commander commanding a set of Soldiers and their equipment (weapons, sensors)
Interface with numerous external command-and-control systems
technical_constraints: Must use a standard set of hardware and software
Communication via encrypted radio modem at 9600 baud (shared channel)
Extreme levels of system robustness required

evaluation_inputs.system_description: The Battlefield Control System (BCS) is a real-time system used by army battalions to control troop movement, strategy, and operations. It supports a Commander who commands a set of Soldiers and their equipment (weapons, sensors). The system interfaces with external command-and-control systems to receive commands and intelligence and to report status. It operates on a client-server model where the Commander node acts as the server and Soldier nodes are clients, communicating exclusively via encrypted messages over a shared 9600 baud radio modem channel.

phase_1_discovery.initial_documentation_status: Minimal; high level data flows and functionality divisions with no clear mapping to software constructs or architectural styles.
evaluator_inquiry_questions: What is the structure of the message-handling software (modules, functions, APIs, layers)?
What facilities exist in the software architecture for self-testing and monitoring of software components?
What facilities exist for redundancy, liveness monitoring, failover, and data consistency maintenance?
What is the process/task view of the system, including mapping to hardware?
What functional dependencies exist among software components ('uses' view)?
What data is kept in the database, how big is it, how much does it change, and who reads/writes it?
What is the anticipated frequency and volume of data being transmitted among system components?

hardware_view_description: The Commander node acts as a server and the Soldier nodes are its clients.
Soldier nodes make requests of the Commander and update the server's database with their status.
Inter-node communication is only through encrypted messages sent via a radio modem.
The radio modem is a shared communication channel; only one node can broadcast at any moment.

questions: How is the failure of a component detected?
How is the failure of a communication channel detected?
What is the latency for a spare component to be turned into a working replacement?
By what means is a failed component marked for replacement?
How are the system's working components notified that they should use the services of the spare?

raw_candidate_scenarios.description: Same information presented to user, but different presentation (location, fonts, sizes, colors, etc.).
Additional data requested to be presented to user.
User requests a change of dialog.
A new device is added to the network, e.g., a location device that returns accurate GPS data.
An existing device adds additional fields that are not currently handled to existing messages.
The time budget for initialization is reduced from 5 minutes to 90 seconds.
Modem baud rate is increased by a factor of 4.
Can a new schedule be accommodated by the OS?.
Change the number of Soldier nodes from 25 to 35.
Change the number of simultaneous missions from 3 to 6.
A node converts from being a Soldier/client to become a Commander/server.

architectural_approaches.description: The system utilizes a client-server model where the Commander node acts as the server (central database and command hub) and the Soldier nodes act as clients (data consumers and status providers).

addressed_attributes.rationale: Centralizes the management of the environmental database and mission plans, allowing for controlled distribution over the limited bandwidth.
Separates the concerns of the commander (strategy/coordination) from the soldiers (execution/sensing), allowing independent updates to client or server logic.

architectural_approaches.description: Communication is strictly performed via encrypted messages sent over a radio modem. This decouples the nodes and handles the physical constraints of the communication medium.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Encryption of the message stream is a primary requirement for battlefield operations to prevent interception.
Discrete messaging allows for the implementation of retry logic and acknowledgement patterns necessary for unreliable radio links.

architectural_approaches.description: A protocol-level approach to manage the shared 9600 baud radio channel, ensuring only one node broadcasts at a time to prevent signal collisions.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Prevents the performance degradation that would occur from packet collisions and subsequent retransmissions on a very slow (9600 baud) channel.
Ensures that the shared communication channel remains usable and is not saturated by conflicting broadcasts.

children.scenario_description: Commander sends 1KB mission plan to 10 soldiers; all receive it within 5 minutes over 9600 baud link.
Enemy intercepts radio signals; cannot decrypt battlefield data within 24 hours using standard computational resources.
Radio link suffers intermittent interference; system retries and delivers soldier status update within 60 seconds.
A new environmental sensor is added to the Soldier node; requires no changes to the Commander node's core communication logic.

scenarios.scenario_text: Commander sends 1KB mission plan to 10 soldiers; all receive it within 5 minutes over 9600 baud link.
Enemy intercepts radio signals; cannot decrypt battlefield data within 24 hours using standard computational resources.
Radio link suffers intermittent interference; system retries and delivers soldier status update within 60 seconds.
A new environmental sensor is added to the Soldier node; requires no changes to the Commander node's core communication logic.

scenario_reference.text: Commander sends 1KB mission plan to 10 soldiers; all receive it within 5 minutes over 9600 baud link.

analysis_records.reasoning: At 9600 baud, effective throughput is approximately 800-1000 bytes/sec after overhead. 10KB of data (1KB per soldier) would take ~10-13 seconds in ideal conditions. The 5-minute window provides a massive buffer for retries and protocol negotiation, making the architecture highly likely to meet this goal.

risks.rationale: If mission plans increase in size or frequency, the 9600 baud link will quickly become a bottleneck, leading to exponential latency increases.

non_risks.rationale: The 5-minute requirement is so lenient relative to the 1KB size that standard protocol overhead (TCP-like ACKs) will not jeopardize the deadline.

scenario_reference.text: Enemy intercepts radio signals; cannot decrypt battlefield data within 24 hours using standard computational resources.

analysis_records.reasoning: The use of encryption on the message stream addresses the confidentiality requirement. However, the strength of the encryption must be balanced against the processing power of the soldier nodes.

risks.rationale: High-strength encryption (e.g., AES-256) may cause significant battery drain or CPU latency on low-power mobile soldier units.

scenario_reference.text: Radio link suffers intermittent interference; system retries and delivers soldier status update within 60 seconds.

analysis_records.stimulus: Transmission failure due to signal noise
analysis_records.reasoning: The discrete messaging approach allows for individual packet retries. The shared-medium control prevents further collisions, which is essential when the signal is already degraded by external interference.

risks.rationale: If multiple nodes experience interference simultaneously, uncoordinated retries could saturate the 9600 baud channel further.

non_risks.rationale: The decoupled message-passing nature ensures that one soldier's connection failure does not prevent others from communicating with the Commander.
